metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief design note to the plan specifying HOW `threshold_for` will dispatch between `default_posture` and `blocking_enabled` conventions (e.g., 'accepts a `gate` enum argument and maps per-gate posture key at call time', or 'reads a per-criterion `posture_key` field in the descriptor').
findings.finding: The plan states `threshold_for` must 'honor BOTH `default_posture` (plan-review) and `blocking_enabled` (code-review) without collapsing the divergence,' but no design is given for how a single function reconciles two structurally different boolean conventions — leaving the executor to invent a non-trivial API design.
findings.location: What section ('Reconcile the deliberate posture divergence in one threshold_for...') and AC2

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Decompose into at minimum 3 child tickets: (1) core `rebar.llm.criteria` layer + threshold_for reconciliation, (2) attestation port to code-review, (3) ADR/docs — each independently mergeable and verifiable.
findings.finding: This story has 7 independent acceptance criteria spanning at least 4 independent concerns (registry unification, posture reconciliation, attestation porting, docs/ADR) with no child-ticket decomposition, violating the single-concern test for a plan of this scope.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the shared `rebar.llm.criteria` descriptor schema explicitly — specifying which fields are required for all tiers, which are tier-gated (e.g. `scenario`/`checklist` for LLM tiers only, `blocking_enabled` for DET tiers only), and how code-review's `applies_to` globs vs plan-review's `applies_at` structured filter co-exist in the unified type.
findings.finding: The shared `rebar.llm.criteria` descriptor schema is never defined in the plan. Plan-review descriptors carry {id, exec, facet, name, scenario, applies_at, checklist, block_threshold, default_posture, routing, trigger, overlay_routing}; code-review routing entries carry {exec, applies_to, default_posture, block_threshold, blocking_enabled} — no facet, no checklist, no applies_at, no scenario. The plan says the shared descriptor must be 'exec-tier-polymorphic (LLM overlay + prompt-less DET)' but never specifies which fields are required, optional, or gated-by-tier in the unified schema.
findings.location: ## What — 'Exec-tier-polymorphic criteria' bullet; AC line 3 ('The shared descriptor is exec-tier-polymorphic (LLM overlay + prompt-less DET)')

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define what `effective_criteria(gate='code_review')` returns (the code-review overlay vocabulary + activated project overlays) and specify the routing fields a project code-review criterion entry must carry in `.rebar/criteria_routing.json` (at minimum: `applies_to` globs for Round-A glob-triggering, `blocking_enabled` for threshold_for).
findings.finding: AC#4 ('Code-review consumes `effective_criteria` (project criteria + `.rebar/` overlay work in code-review too)') references `effective_criteria` — a plan-review function (plan_review/registry.py:294) tightly coupled to `CANONICAL_LLM` and the plan-review overlay schema. But code-review's criterion vocabulary is `OVERLAY_IDS` (11 specialist overlays) plus 2 DET security keys — a completely non-overlapping set. The plan never defines what `effective_criteria` means in the shared context: does it return plan-review ids, code-review overlay ids, or both? How does `.rebar/criteria_routing.json` map project criteria to code-review overlays (which have `applies_to` globs, not `applies_at` level filters)?
findings.location: AC line 4: 'Code-review consumes effective_criteria (project criteria + .rebar/ overlay work in code-review too)'; ## Scope — 'code_review/registry.py (delegate to it)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the `threshold_for` merge rule explicitly: e.g. 'blocking = (`default_posture == "blocking"` for the plan-review path) OR (`blocking_enabled == true` for the code-review path); the shared function takes a `gate` parameter to select the right field, keeping the two conventions structurally separate rather than merged into a single expression.'
findings.finding: The new unified `threshold_for` reconciliation rule is unspecified when BOTH posture conventions are present. The plan says it 'honors BOTH `default_posture` (plan-review) AND `blocking_enabled` (code-review)' but never states the merge precedence. When a shared-registry entry carries both fields (e.g. a project overlay criterion activated on both gates), which governs blocking: `default_posture == 'blocking'` OR `blocking_enabled == true`? Are they ANDed, ORed, or does one gate's path use one field and the other uses the other?
findings.location: ## What — 'Reconcile the deliberate posture divergence in one threshold_for' bullet; AC line 2 ('threshold_for honors BOTH posture conventions')
findings.suggested_fix: The lowest-ambiguity design: the shared `threshold_for` accepts a `gate: Literal['plan_review', 'code_review']` argument and dispatches to the appropriate field — plan-review uses `default_posture`, code-review uses `blocking_enabled`. This preserves the deliberate divergence structurally rather than by convention.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a testing plan section (or test ACs) covering at minimum: (a) unit tests for `threshold_for` under both posture conventions and conflict cases, (b) an integration test exercising the rollback/fail-open path with a missing overlay, and (c) a test confirming the DET prompt-less tier dispatches correctly.
findings.finding: The plan introduces substantial new testable logic (threshold_for posture reconciliation, exec-tier-polymorphic descriptor loading, expand-contract migration rollback path, attestation porting) but contains zero testing language — no unit tests, integration tests, or test strategy of any kind.
findings.location: What section; Acceptance Criteria; Scope section
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Scope: 'Tests: unit tests for threshold_for covering default_posture-only, blocking_enabled-only, both-set, and neither-set cases; an integration test confirming overlay-absent fallback returns per-gate legacy behavior; a test for exec-tier dispatch (LLM vs DET).  AC: All new paths have ≥1 failure-path test.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact basis for the code-review `registry_version` stamp (AC6) — e.g., whether it derives from the unified `rebar.llm.criteria` shared vocabulary (preferred, so plan-review and code-review both stamp against the same registry) or from a code-review-specific combination of `OVERLAY_IDS` + `routing_index()` — and confirm it covers the vocabulary changes that must invalidate a stale code-review attestation.
findings.finding: The plan asserts code-review attestations will carry 'a code-review `registry_version`' (AC6), but the existing `registry_version()` in `attest.py` (lines 37-57) is grounded in plan-review-specific symbols (`CANONICAL_DET`, `CANONICAL_LLM`, `CODEBASE_GROUNDED`, `_routing_index()`) that have no direct code-review analogs — code-review has `OVERLAY_IDS` and `routing_index()` only. After unification into `rebar.llm.criteria`, the plan does not specify what basis the code-review `registry_version` stamp will use, leaving the drift-detection correctness of code-review attestations (the 'registry_unchanged' precondition in remediation-mode and claim-gate checks) unverified.
findings.location: AC6: 'Code-review issues kind-keyed attestations via a ported `attest.py` with a code-review `registry_version`'; `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/attest.py:37-57` (`registry_version()`).
findings.suggested_fix: After unifying into `rebar.llm.criteria`, define a single `criteria.registry_version(gate='plan_review'|'code_review')` that stamps the gate's active vocabulary (for plan-review: `CANONICAL_DET + CANONICAL_LLM + routing_index`; for code-review: `OVERLAY_IDS + routing_index`). This preserves per-gate invalidation granularity while giving both gates a well-specified, gate-scoped stamp drawn from the same shared layer — no cross-gate leakage of invalidation signals.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that the 'fail-open to packaged' fallback for code-review MUST preserve `blocking_enabled=True` for `secret-detection` and `high-critical-security` — i.e. the DET security posture is hardcoded as a floor in `detectors.py`/`threshold_for` and cannot be overridden to advisory by a misbehaving shared registry.
findings.finding: The plan's fallback specification ('A misbehaving unified registry falls back per-gate') does not define whether the DET security criteria's fail-closed posture is preserved on fallback. Code-review's `secret-detection` and `high-critical-security` entries (`blocking_enabled: true`, `block_threshold: 0.5`) are NOT in `OVERLAY_IDS` — they are a separate DET subsystem (detectors.py) that must block. If the shared registry 'falls back to packaged' and the DET entries are missing/dropped from the fallback path, the fail-closed invariant WS5 established is silently broken.
findings.location: ## Scope — 'Migration is expand-contract; rollback = fail-open to packaged (overlay absent ⇒ both gates behave as today). A misbehaving unified registry falls back per-gate.'
findings.suggested_fix: In detectors.py, make the fail-closed floor a hardcoded constant rather than a routing lookup: `blocking_enabled = registry.threshold_for([crit])[1] or crit in (SECRET_DETECTION, HIGH_CRITICAL_SECURITY)` — the `or` clause ensures the DET security criteria block even if the unified registry fails to carry their entries.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a RED fixture or parametrized unit test exercising `threshold_for` across all four combinations of `default_posture`/`blocking_enabled` presence (both present, only one, neither) AND add an observable signal (log/metric) for the per-gate fallback path before full build-out.
findings.finding: The dual-posture merge in `threshold_for` (`default_posture` vs `blocking_enabled`) and the per-gate fallback ('A misbehaving unified registry falls back per-gate') are asserted correct with no empirical validation step — no RED fixture, spike, or property-based test is planned to verify edge cases (e.g., a criterion carrying both fields, a criterion carrying neither, fallback triggering silently).
findings.location: What section — 'Reconcile the deliberate posture divergence in one `threshold_for`'; Scope — 'A misbehaving unified registry falls back per-gate'
findings.suggested_fix: Write a pytest parametrize table covering the four field-presence combinations and assert the expected blocking bool for each; add a structured log line (e.g., `logger.warning('criteria.fallback', gate=gate)`) in the fallback branch so it is detectable in CI and production.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the contradiction between AC1 ('replacing the two parallel registries') and the Scope section ('delegate to it'): explicitly state whether old registry files are deleted, kept as empty shims, or retained with thin delegation — and update AC1 to match.
findings.finding: AC1 says the shared layer 'replac[es] the two parallel registries,' but the Scope section says the existing per-gate registry files remain and 'delegate to it' — these two signals directly contradict each other on whether the old files are removed or kept as thin shims.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria AC1 vs Scope section ('plan_review/registry.py + code_review/registry.py (delegate to it)')
findings.suggested_fix: Rewrite AC1 as: 'A shared rebar.llm.criteria layer that both plan-review and code-review consume; per-gate registry.py files are retained as thin delegation shims (expand-contract pattern, no import-path breakage).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify a concrete rollback mechanism (e.g., a per-gate env var or config flag that switches back to the per-gate registry), and document the deploy ordering: b744 WS6 live → shared criteria deployed → plan-review switched → code-review switched (each independently verifiable).
findings.finding: The rollback mechanism is asserted ('rollback = fail-open to packaged; overlay absent ⇒ both gates behave as today') but no concrete trigger, feature flag, or tested fallback path is described, and the deploy ordering across the two gates and the b744 blocker is unspecified — yet the plan changes runtime behavior of three deployed consumers (MCP `review_code`, CLI, Gerrit `review_bot/adapter.py`).
findings.location: Scope section — 'Migration is expand-contract; rollback = fail-open to packaged'; AC item 4; 'BLOCKED ON b744'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `REBAR_UNIFIED_CRITERIA=1` env flag consumed by both gate registries; when absent, each gate falls back to its existing loader. Document the ordered deploy sequence in the ADR so ops can toggle per-gate independently.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the fate of `criteria_routing.json` in the Scope (retire and redirect docs, keep as delegate, or explicitly migrate its content) so that existing references are not stranded after unification.
findings.finding: The plan does not specify the fate of `criteria_routing.json` — whether it is retired, kept, or migrated — after the unified registry subsumes its content (including the 2 DET security keys). Any existing docs or references to this file path could be stranded stale after the unification lands.
findings.location: Scope / What section ('2 security keys live only in code-review's criteria_routing.json today')
findings.suggested_fix: Add a bullet to Scope: 'Retire `code_review/criteria_routing.json`: migrate its entries (including the 2 DET security-key invariants) into the shared descriptor format in `src/rebar/llm/criteria/`; update any CLAUDE.md/docs references to point to the new location.' This both completes the expand-contract for that artifact and prevents stale-reference T7(b) issues.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at least one AC with a concrete validation mechanism (e.g., a before/after behavioral comparison showing both gates reach identical threshold decisions for a shared criterion, or an integration smoke-test that exercises the unified registry end-to-end across both gates).
findings.finding: No concrete validation mechanism exists for any AC — all 7 criteria are purely structural/internal system properties (shared layer exists, posture reconciliation present, ADR written) with no before/after workflow comparison, operational metric, or dogfooding criterion that would confirm the unification actually improves outcomes for consumers.
findings.suggested_fix: Add AC: 'A shared integration test confirms that for a criterion present in both gates, `threshold_for` returns the same effective posture as the legacy per-gate lookup did before unification, under both `default_posture` and `blocking_enabled` conventions.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Rewrite AC7 as a specific observable outcome: e.g., 'An ADR exists at docs/adr/NNN-unified-criteria.md covering decision context, posture conventions, and exec tiers; CLAUDE.md or docs/ includes a step-by-step how-to for authoring a new criterion, activating it per gate, and verifying it blocks as expected.'
findings.finding: AC7 ('An ADR + CLAUDE.md/docs/ how-to documents the unified registry across gates') is effort-framed ('documents...') rather than stating a specific observable outcome, and does not specify what the ADR must cover — making it non-verifiable whether the deliverable is complete.

findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the concurrency-safety model for `rebar.llm.criteria` initialization: document whether it is a module-level singleton (relying on the GIL/import lock), an explicit threading.Lock-guarded lazy-load, or a stateless re-read-per-call, and confirm idempotency under parallel invocation.
findings.finding: The plan introduces `rebar.llm.criteria` as a shared registry consumed by both gates and by the `.rebar/` overlay loader, but specifies no concurrency-safety guarantee for the initialization path — two concurrent gate invocations (e.g., a parallel CI plan-review + code-review run) could race to load and populate the shared registry without any described lock, CAS, or idempotency guarantee.
findings.suggested_fix: Declare the registry as a module-level constant built eagerly at import time (load once, never mutate), so Python's import lock provides the atomicity guarantee; if lazy loading is required, use `functools.lru_cache` or a `threading.Lock`-guarded init sentinel.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the retirement/invalidation contract for the shared criteria registry: specify whether it is process-scoped (reload on restart only), whether a file-mtime or `registry_version` check triggers a reload, and document this in the ADR so operators know when to restart gates after an overlay change.
findings.finding: The plan describes no retirement, invalidation, or version-check path for the shared criteria registry. If `criteria_routing.json` or the `.rebar/` overlay file changes between process invocations (or within a long-running MCP server process), there is no defined mechanism to detect staleness and reload.
findings.location: Scope section — 'Migration is expand-contract; rollback = fail-open to packaged'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
